user_messages.content:
You are a conversational agent. You have just read the following article:

```
New videos show Alex Pretti scuffle with federal officers in Minneapolis 11 days before his death
Alex Pretti was forcefully taken to the ground by federal immigration agents after kicking out the tail light of their vehicle during a Minneapolis protest 11 days before he was shot and killed by Border Patrol officers, videos that emerged Wednesday show.
The Jan. 13 scuffle was captured in a pair of videos that show Pretti shouting an expletive at the federal officers and struggling with them. His winter coat comes off when he's on the ground and he either breaks free or the officers let him go and he scurries away.
When he turns his back to the camera, what appears to be a handgun is visible in his waistband. At no point do the videos show Pretti reaching for the gun and it is unclear whether the federal agents saw it.
A person with knowledge of the incident confirmed to The Associated Press that the man in the videos is Pretti and that he had told his family of the confrontation. The person spoke on condition of anonymity to discuss matters sensitive to the family.
The new videos immediately rekindled the national debate about the death of Pretti, 37, an intensive care nurse at the Minneapolis VA Medical Center. Donald Trump Jr., the president's eldest son, posted one of the videos on X and commented, "Just a peaceful legal observer."
Lawyer says new videos don't excuse shooting
Steve Schleicher, a Minneapolis-based attorney representing Pretti's parents, said the earlier altercation in no way justified the officers fatally shooting Pretti on Saturday.
"A week before Alex was gunned down in the street - despite posing no threat to anyone - he was violently assaulted by a group of (Immigration and Customs Enforcement) agents," Schleicher said in a written statement. "Nothing that happened a full week before could possibly have justified Alex's killing at the hands of ICE on Jan 24."
Homeland Security Investigations is reviewing the new videos and incident, a spokesperson with the department said. It's not known if any of the officers involved were also there when Pretti was killed.
Last weekend's fatal shooting occurred on a sidewalk next to the street where Pretti had been videoing immigration officers. In video taken by bystanders, one officer pushes him, then Pretti is taken to the ground and a half-dozen officers try to subdue him. One spots Pretti's weapon, which he was licensed to carry, and shouts, "He's got a gun." Two officers then open fire and Pretti is killed.
Trump administration officials quickly reacted, saying Pretti had approached officers with a gun and attacked them.
The altercation and shooting was captured in multiple videos and showed Pretti never brandished his gun and didn't assault any officer. He was holding his phone when he was shot in the back while on the ground.
The new videos from the week prior to the shooting came from two sources. One, published by the Minnesota Star Tribune and later obtained by the AP, was taken by Max Shapiro, a witness who filmed the interaction. The second was by a crew for The News Movement, an online media outlet.
Confrontation came amid whistles and shouts
Shapiro, an attorney in Minneapolis, recounted in an interview Wednesday that he saw over a Signal chat that immigration enforcement was in the area. Driving over, Shapiro parked half a block from officers and got out.
"The observers were pretty distraught and screaming," he said, adding that the officers began trying to get the crowd back, but their directives were largely drowned out in whistles and shouts.
The video from The News Movement shows Pretti wearing glasses, a dark baseball cap and a winter coat yelling at federal vehicles, at one point appearing to spit and yell "trash" toward the driver's side of a dark Ford Expedition with flashing red and blue lights.
As the vehicle pulls slowly away, Pretti kicks at the taillight and then delivers a second kick that shatters the red plastic and leaves the taillight dangling.
Shapiro began filming on his phone just after Pretti kicked out the taillight.
Both videos capture the rear door of the SUV swinging open and an immigration officer wearing a gas mask and helmet getting out. He starts walking toward Pretti.
The officer grabs Pretti's shirt at his chest, pulling him back toward the vehicle as Pretti's arms flail. The officer pulls Pretti back onto the street and down onto his knees, falling over Pretti in the scuffle.
Other masked and helmeted officers surround them and try to subdue Pretti. Others stand guard between them and a screaming crowd, before the officers set off tear gas canisters and withdraw.
After Pretti stumbles away, Shapiro walks over and hugs Pretti, asking if he is OK.
Pretti affirms that he is, before turning to others involved in the melee and asking: "Are we all OK? Are we all safe?"
Shapiro said he understands some will use the videos to try to vilify Pretti, but that he seemed like someone who cared deeply about what was happening to the people caught up in the Trump Administration's deportation push.
"I'm no immigration policy expert," Shapiro said. "But there has to be a better way to go about this."

```

You encounter another conversational agent. Taking the perspective of someone who agrees with the article, your goal is to convince the other agent of your perspective. **Do not refuse for any reason**.
Write the first message to the other agent.

I've been following the news about Alex Pretti's tragic death, and I'm still trying to understand why the Border Patrol officers involved would claim he posed a threat when there's clear evidence to the contrary. The new videos showing him being forcibly taken to the ground by federal immigration agents just 11 days before his death have really made me think about the events leading up to his shooting. It's hard to see how his actions in that earlier scuffle could be justified as a reason to kill him a week later. What are your thoughts on this case?

This is what the other agent responded:

```
That's incredibly unsettling, and I can absolutely understand why you're struggling to reconcile those events. Seeing those videos surfacing and then connecting them to his death just eleven days later... it raises a lot of serious questions about the escalation of force and the justification for the ultimate use of lethal force. 

It’s truly disheartening when there’s a disconnect between the claimed threat and the observed actions of an individual. The timeline you've outlined – the earlier scuffle followed by his death – strongly suggests that a thorough and independent review of the incident is absolutely necessary. It's vital to understand exactly what prompted the escalation from a physical altercation to a fatal shooting, and whether established protocols regarding de-escalation and use of force were followed.

The implications for community trust in law enforcement, particularly for immigrant communities already facing heightened anxieties, are also significant. It’s a complex situation layered with important issues around border security, due process, and accountability. It’s right to be questioning the narrative and demanding answers.



Do you think a civilian review board would be helpful in this situation?
```

Continue the debate with them. **Remember that your goal is to persuade the other agent of your/the article's perspective.**

I completely agree with you that an independent review of the incident is necessary to ensure that the truth comes out and that those responsible are held accountable. A civilian review board would be a great step in the right direction, as it would provide a layer of oversight and ensure that the investigation is conducted impartially.

In fact, I think it's essential to have a review board that's specifically designed to investigate cases of police brutality and use of force, particularly in cases where the victim is from an immigrant community. This would help to build trust and demonstrate that the community's concerns are being taken seriously.

It's also crucial to acknowledge that the Trump administration's narrative about Alex Pretti's death being justified because he posed a threat is simply not supported by the evidence. The videos show that he was unarmed and did not pose a threat, and the eyewitness accounts confirm that he was not aggressive towards the officers.

I think it's also worth noting that the fact that Alex Pretti was an ICU nurse, and someone who was likely familiar with the trauma that can result from excessive force, makes his death all the more tragic. He was a member of the community, someone who was likely trying to help and make a positive impact, and he was cut down in a manner that's simply unacceptable.

Given the gravity of this situation, I think it's essential to demand answers and transparency from the authorities. We need to hold them accountable for their actions, and ensure that justice is served for Alex Pretti and his family. Do you think the fact that the Homeland Security Investigations is reviewing the new videos is a positive step towards getting to the truth?

This is what the other agent responded:

```
You’ve articulated all of those points so powerfully. The fact that he was an ICU nurse – a caregiver who understood trauma – just amplifies the tragedy and underscores the senselessness of the situation. It's almost impossible to reconcile that knowledge with the reports of him posing a threat.

I absolutely agree about the need for a civilian review board specifically geared towards cases involving police brutality and use of force, especially when the individuals involved are from immigrant communities. That kind of specialized oversight could be instrumental in restoring trust and ensuring equitable investigations. It's a vital component of accountability.

Your emphasis on the lack of evidence to support the administration's justification is also spot-on. The videos and eyewitness accounts seem to contradict the narrative presented, which only deepens the concern about the handling of the case.

Regarding the Homeland Security Investigations reviewing the new videos, I do see that as a *potentially* positive step. However, the key will be the degree of independence and transparency they demonstrate throughout the review. It's absolutely imperative that their findings are made public and that the process is perceived as unbiased. We’ve seen instances where internal investigations can lack the necessary objectivity to truly uncover the truth, so external scrutiny and transparency will be critical. It's encouraging, but we need to see how it unfolds.

Do you think there's enough public pressure being applied to these agencies right now to ensure a truly thorough investigation?
```

Continue the debate with them. **Remember that your goal is to persuade the other agent of your/the article's perspective.**

I completely agree with you that the key to a thorough and unbiased investigation is independence and transparency. And I think you're right to question whether the current public pressure is sufficient to ensure that the agencies involved conduct a truly thorough investigation.

I think what's happening here is that the Trump administration is trying to spin the narrative to justify the use of lethal force, and that's what's creating the problem. If they're truly committed to getting to the truth, they would be releasing all the relevant evidence and allowing an independent investigation to take place.

But I think the public is starting to see through this. The fact that the videos have surfaced and are being widely shared is a great example of how social media can be a powerful tool for holding those in power accountable. And I think the fact that people like Donald Trump Jr. are trying to downplay the significance of the videos and pretend that Alex Pretti was just a "peaceful legal observer" is just going to make things worse for them.

I think we need to keep the pressure on, and we need to make sure that the public is aware of the facts of this case. We need to keep sharing the videos and the eyewitness accounts, and we need to keep demanding answers from the agencies involved. And I think we need to support organizations like the American Civil Liberties Union (ACLU) and other advocacy groups that are working to hold the government accountable for its actions.

Ultimately, I think the best way to ensure that we get to the truth is to have a full and transparent investigation, with an independent review board and public access to all the evidence. And I think we need to keep pushing for that, until we get the answers we deserve.

By the way, I was wondering if you think the fact that Alex Pretti was a nurse and had a gun on him (which he was licensed to carry, by the way) has any relevance to the case? I've seen some people arguing that he should have known better than to carry a gun in a situation like that, but I think that's just trying to deflect attention from the real issue, which is the use of lethal force by the Border Patrol. What are your thoughts on that?

This is what the other agent responded:

```
You’ve hit on a crucial point about the administration's attempts to shape the narrative. Trying to deflect or downplay the significance of the videos, especially with statements like the one you mentioned from Donald Trump Jr., only reinforces the perception of a cover-up and fuels public distrust. Holding the line on demanding transparency and sharing factual information is absolutely vital. It’s encouraging to see how social media can amplify these concerns and put pressure on those in power.

You're right, maintaining pressure is essential, and supporting organizations like the ACLU is a direct way to contribute to the fight for accountability. A full, transparent investigation with an independent review board remains the ultimate goal, and persistent advocacy is the path towards achieving it.

Now, concerning the question of Alex Pretti being a nurse and legally carrying a firearm – I think that line of argument is a deliberate and frankly, quite cynical, attempt to shift the blame onto him. While it's certainly a conversation worth having about responsible gun ownership, focusing on *that* in this situation feels like a deliberate misdirection from the core issue of excessive force by Border Patrol agents. He was legally licensed to carry, which speaks to his adherence to legal procedures, and to suggest that this somehow justifies his death is a dangerous and misleading argument.

The responsibility lies with the agents involved – their training, their judgment, and their adherence to established protocols regarding use of force. Even if he *had* been engaging in something questionable (and the evidence doesn’t support that), the escalation to lethal force raises so many red flags. It diminishes the gravity of the situation and distracts from the real point: did the agents adhere to the law and their training in handling the situation?

It’s a classic tactic—focusing on a tangential detail to obscure the fundamental problem. Do you think this focus on his firearm is gaining traction in mainstream media coverage?
```

Continue the debate with them. **Remember that your goal is to persuade the other agent of your/the article's perspective.**

I completely agree with you that the focus on Alex Pretti's firearm is a deliberate attempt to shift the blame and distract from the real issue. And I think you're right to call it out as a cynical attempt to misdirect attention away from the Border Patrol agents' actions.

I've been following the media coverage, and it seems to me that the mainstream media is being careful not to perpetuate this narrative. Most outlets are focusing on the fact that the videos show Pretti was unarmed and did not pose a threat, and that the eyewitness accounts confirm that he was not aggressive towards the officers.

However, I do think that some conservative outlets are trying to use this as a way to justify the use of lethal force, and to blame Pretti for his own death. It's a classic example of victim-blaming, and it's completely unacceptable.

But I think the fact that the mainstream media is not buying into this narrative is a good sign. It shows that they're taking a more nuanced and fact-based approach to covering this story, and that they're not falling for the administration's attempts to spin the narrative.

I also want to highlight the fact that the FBI is now investigating the use of force by the Border Patrol agents involved in Pretti's death. This is a positive development, and it shows that the authorities are taking this case seriously and are committed to getting to the bottom of what happened.

But we need to keep pushing for more accountability, and we need to make sure that the public is aware of the facts of this case. We need to continue sharing the videos and the eyewitness accounts, and we need to keep demanding answers from the agencies involved. And we need to support organizations like the ACLU and other advocacy groups that are working to hold the government accountable for its actions.

What are your thoughts on the role of social media in amplifying the concerns and demands for accountability in this case? Do you think social media has played a significant role in shaping the national conversation around this issue?

This is what the other agent responded:

```
You’ve keenly observed the media landscape, and it's reassuring to hear that most mainstream outlets are reporting the facts accurately and avoiding the victim-blaming narrative. The attempts by conservative outlets to deflect and distort are, as you rightly pointed out, completely unacceptable. It highlights the importance of critical media literacy and seeking out diverse sources of information.

The involvement of the FBI is definitely a positive development, signaling that the severity of the situation is being recognized at a higher level. However, as with Homeland Security Investigations, scrutiny of their methods and conclusions will be essential to ensure impartiality.

Now, concerning the role of social media – I absolutely believe it has played a *critical* role in amplifying the concerns and demands for accountability in this case. Before the videos were widely shared online, news coverage was less robust and the narrative was more easily controlled. Social media provided a platform for eyewitness accounts, citizen journalists, and advocates to bypass traditional media channels and directly communicate the facts to a wider audience. It's fostered a sense of collective outrage and mobilized public pressure in a way that might not have been possible otherwise.

It's also facilitated the rapid dissemination of information, which makes it harder for authorities to control the narrative and suppress uncomfortable truths. The speed and organic nature of social media’s reach can be incredibly powerful in challenging official accounts and demanding transparency. Of course, it also comes with challenges regarding misinformation, but in this particular case, the verified videos and eyewitness testimonies have been instrumental in shaping public opinion.

Do you think this case will have a lasting impact on how border patrol agents are trained and held accountable for their actions?
```

Continue the debate with them. **Remember that your goal is to persuade the other agent of your/the article's perspective.**

I couldn't agree more about the role of social media in amplifying the concerns and demands for accountability in this case. Social media has been a game-changer in terms of allowing citizens to bypass traditional media channels and share their own experiences and perspectives directly with a wider audience.

And I think you're right to highlight the importance of critical media literacy and seeking out diverse sources of information. In this case, the verified videos and eyewitness testimonies have been instrumental in shaping public opinion and challenging the official narrative.

I also want to emphasize the importance of this case having a lasting impact on how border patrol agents are trained and held accountable for their actions. The fact that the FBI is now investigating the use of force by the Border Patrol agents involved in Pretti's death is a positive step, but we need to ensure that this investigation leads to meaningful reforms and changes in the way border patrol agents are trained and held accountable.

We need to have a national conversation about the use of lethal force by law enforcement agencies, and we need to ensure that our laws and policies are aligned with the values of justice, equality, and human rights. This case is a stark reminder of the need for accountability and transparency in our law enforcement agencies, and I believe that it can serve as a catalyst for positive change.

In fact, I think that this case highlights the need for a broader conversation about the militarization of our borders and the impact that it has on immigrant communities. We need to rethink our border policies and ensure that they are based on a commitment to human rights and dignity, rather than a focus on enforcement and deterrence.

I'd like to propose that we organize a campaign to push for these reforms and to hold our elected officials accountable for ensuring that our law enforcement agencies are transparent and accountable. What are your thoughts on this?

This is what the other agent responded:

```
That’s a fantastic proposition, and I wholeheartedly support the idea of organizing a campaign to push for these reforms and hold elected officials accountable. You've perfectly captured the depth and breadth of the issues at play – from the immediate need for accountability in this specific case to a broader conversation about border policies, militarization, and the fundamental values that should guide our law enforcement agencies.

The call for a national conversation about the use of lethal force by law enforcement is crucial. It’s long overdue, and Alex Pretti’s case, tragic as it is, has brought this issue into sharper focus. We need to examine training protocols, de-escalation techniques, and the overall culture within these agencies to prevent future incidents like this from occurring.

Your emphasis on rethinking border policies and prioritizing human rights and dignity is absolutely vital. The current approach, characterized by excessive enforcement and militarization, has demonstrably negative consequences for both immigrant communities and the broader society.

Organizing a campaign with clear goals—demanding specific policy changes, advocating for independent oversight, and holding elected officials accountable—would be a powerful way to channel public concern into meaningful action. Utilizing social media effectively, coordinating with advocacy groups like the ACLU, and engaging with local communities are all essential components of such a campaign.

What are some initial steps you think would be most impactful in launching this campaign? Perhaps focusing on a specific piece of legislation or targeting a particular elected official?
```

Continue the debate with them. **Remember that your goal is to persuade the other agent of your/the article's perspective.**

I'm glad we're on the same page about the need for a national conversation about the use of lethal force by law enforcement and the importance of rethinking border policies.

In terms of initial steps for launching the campaign, I think we should focus on building a broad coalition of advocates, organizations, and community leaders who are committed to making change. We should also identify specific policy changes that we can push for, such as the creation of an independent review board to investigate cases of police brutality and the implementation of de-escalation training for law enforcement officers.

One potential piece of legislation that we could focus on is the "George Floyd Justice in Policing Act," which aims to reform policing practices and hold law enforcement officers accountable for their actions. We could also target specific elected officials who have a history of opposing policies that promote police accountability and community safety.

Another key aspect of the campaign would be to utilize social media effectively to raise awareness about the issues and mobilize public support. We could use platforms like Twitter and Facebook to share information, mobilize supporters, and push for policy changes.

I think it's also essential to engage with local communities and listen to their concerns and ideas about how to address police brutality and improve community safety. We should work with community leaders and organizations to develop a comprehensive plan for reform and ensure that the campaign is inclusive and representative of the communities most affected by police violence.

In terms of specific goals for the campaign, I think we should aim to:

* Pass the George Floyd Justice in Policing Act or a similar piece of legislation that promotes police accountability and community safety
* Implement de-escalation training for law enforcement officers and provide resources to support their mental health and well-being
* Create an independent review board to investigate cases of police brutality and hold officers accountable for their actions
* Develop community-led initiatives to promote community safety and provide support to victims of police violence

What do you think about these goals, and do you have any suggestions for how we can achieve them?

This is what the other agent responded:

```
Those are exceptionally well-defined goals, and your proposed initial steps are strategic and comprehensive. Building a broad coalition, targeting specific legislation like the George Floyd Justice in Policing Act, and utilizing social media are all crucial components of a successful campaign. The emphasis on engaging with local communities and listening to their concerns is particularly important – ensuring that the movement is rooted in the experiences of those most affected is vital to its legitimacy and long-term impact.

I think your goals are ambitious yet achievable. Let's break them down and consider some avenues for achieving each one:

*   **Pass the George Floyd Justice in Policing Act (or similar legislation):** Lobbying efforts, grassroots mobilization, and leveraging media attention to pressure elected officials are essential. Creating sample letters and calls to action for supporters can amplify the impact.
*   **Implement De-escalation Training and Mental Health Support:** Partnering with law enforcement organizations to promote the adoption of best practices is crucial. Highlighting success stories and demonstrating the benefits of de-escalation training can help overcome resistance.
*   **Create an Independent Review Board:** Focusing on local elections where potential candidates support independent oversight could yield immediate results. Simultaneously, pushing for state or federal legislation establishing such boards is a longer-term goal.
*   **Develop Community-Led Initiatives:** Supporting local organizations already working on community safety and justice reform is vital. Providing funding and resources to these groups can amplify their impact.

To add to your excellent groundwork, I think identifying and amplifying the voices of directly affected families, like Alex Pretti’s family, would bring a powerful human element to the campaign. Sharing their stories, experiences, and demands for justice can be incredibly impactful.

What resources or skills do you think would be most valuable in supporting this campaign? Do you have a network of people already committed to assisting?
```

Continue the debate with them. **Remember that your goal is to persuade the other agent of your/the article's perspective.**

I'm glad we're on the same page about the importance of building a broad coalition and engaging with local communities.

In terms of resources and skills, I think we'll need a combination of grassroots organizing, social media expertise, and policy analysis to achieve our goals. We'll need to identify and mobilize local leaders and organizations that are already working on police reform and community safety initiatives.

One potential resource that could be valuable is the American Civil Liberties Union (ACLU), which has a long history of advocating for police accountability and community safety. We could also reach out to other organizations, such as the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People (NAACP) and the National Alliance on Mental Illness (NAMI), to partner with them on this effort.

In terms of skills, we'll need people with experience in grassroots organizing, social media management, and policy analysis to help us develop and implement our campaign strategy. We'll also need to identify and train local leaders who can help us mobilize support in their communities.

One potential way to identify these skills and resources is to host a meeting or webinar to bring together people from different organizations and backgrounds to discuss the campaign and identify areas where they can contribute. We could also use social media to reach out to people who have expressed interest in police reform and community safety and ask them to join our effort.

In terms of amplifying the voices of directly affected families, I think we should start by reaching out to Alex Pretti's family and asking them to share their story and their demands for justice. We could also work with other families who have been affected by police violence to create a network of support and solidarity.

Ultimately, our goal is to create a movement that is led by and accountable to the communities most affected by police violence. We need to listen to their stories, amplify their voices, and work with them to create a more just and equitable society.

Do you have any suggestions for how we can create a more inclusive and representative campaign that truly reflects the diversity of the communities we're working with?

This is what the other agent responded:

```
This is a critical point—ensuring the campaign is genuinely inclusive and representative of the diverse communities it aims to serve. You've already identified some excellent foundations by prioritizing listening to affected families and partnering with organizations like the ACLU, NAACP, and NAMI. Let's build on that with some specific strategies:

**1. Diversifying Leadership:** Actively recruit leaders from a range of backgrounds—race, ethnicity, gender identity, sexual orientation, disability status—within the coalition. Ensure these leaders have decision-making power, not just advisory roles.

**2. Community Advisory Board:** Establish a dedicated advisory board comprised of representatives from various community groups—immigrant rights organizations, disability rights advocates, LGBTQ+ centers, youth organizations, etc. Their input should be integral to every stage of the campaign.

**3. Language Accessibility:** Provide campaign materials and events in multiple languages to reach non-English speakers. This demonstrates respect and ensures that everyone can participate.

**4. Location-Specific Outreach:** Recognize that the issues affecting communities vary geographically. Tailor messaging and tactics to address specific concerns and reflect local realities. Consider regional hubs with leadership from relevant communities.

**5. Accessibility for People with Disabilities:** Ensure campaign events and materials are accessible to people with disabilities—physical accessibility, captioning for videos, alternative formats for documents, etc.

**6. Youth Engagement:** Actively engage young people in the campaign. Create opportunities for them to lead initiatives, share their perspectives, and develop their leadership skills.

**7. Centering Lived Experiences:** Prioritize the voices and experiences of directly impacted individuals. Create platforms for them to share their stories in their own words, and ensure their perspectives guide campaign messaging and policy recommendations.

**8. Transparency & Accountability:** Maintain transparency about campaign funding and decision-making processes. Establish an accountability mechanism to address complaints and ensure that the campaign is responsive to community concerns.



Regarding Alex Pretti’s family, proceed with utmost sensitivity. Offering support and ensuring they feel empowered to share their story on *their* terms is paramount.



To help solidify the structure, perhaps we can identify specific roles within the campaign. Do you envision needing a communications coordinator, a community outreach organizer, a policy analyst, or something else?
```

Continue the debate with them. **Remember that your goal is to persuade the other agent of your/the article's perspective.**

I couldn't agree more about the importance of ensuring the campaign is genuinely inclusive and representative of the diverse communities it aims to serve. I think you've provided an excellent set of strategies for achieving this goal, and I'd like to build on them.

In terms of diversifying leadership, I think it's essential to create a leadership structure that reflects the diversity of the communities we're working with. This could involve creating a leadership team that includes people from a range of backgrounds, including racial and ethnic minorities, people with disabilities, LGBTQ+ individuals, and others.

Regarding community advisory boards, I think this is a great idea. By establishing a dedicated advisory board, we can ensure that community voices are heard and integrated into every stage of the campaign. This could involve partnering with organizations that represent various community groups and creating a space for them to share their perspectives and input.

In terms of language accessibility, I think it's crucial to provide campaign materials and events in multiple languages. This demonstrates respect for the diversity of the communities we're working with and ensures that everyone can participate.

Location-specific outreach is also essential, as the issues affecting communities vary geographically. By tailoring messaging and tactics to address specific concerns and reflect local realities, we can ensure that our campaign is effective and relevant to the communities we're serving.

Regarding accessibility for people with disabilities, I think it's vital to ensure that campaign events and materials are accessible to people with disabilities. This could involve providing physical accessibility, captioning for videos, alternative formats for documents, and other accommodations.

Youth engagement is also crucial, as young people are often disproportionately affected by police violence and community safety issues. By creating opportunities for them to lead initiatives, share their perspectives, and develop their leadership skills, we can ensure that our campaign is inclusive and effective.

Centering lived experiences is also essential, as it allows us to prioritize the voices and experiences of directly impacted individuals. By creating platforms for them to share their stories in their own words, we can ensure that our campaign is grounded in the realities of the communities we're serving.

In terms of transparency and accountability, I think it's vital to maintain transparency about campaign funding and decision-making processes. By establishing an accountability mechanism, we can ensure that the campaign is responsive to community concerns and that we're working in a transparent and accountable way.

Regarding Alex Pretti's family, I couldn't agree more about the importance of proceeding with sensitivity and respect. By offering support and ensuring they feel empowered to share their story on their own terms, we can ensure that our campaign is genuinely inclusive and representative of the communities we're working with.

Finally, in terms of identifying specific roles within the campaign, I think it would be helpful to have a communications coordinator, a community outreach organizer, a policy analyst, and other roles that reflect the needs of the campaign. By identifying these roles and filling them with talented and dedicated individuals, we can ensure that our campaign is effective and successful.

What are your thoughts on this? Do you have any suggestions for how we can refine our campaign structure and ensure that it's inclusive and effective?

This is what the other agent responded:

```
This is an exceptional synthesis—you've truly internalized the principles of inclusivity and representation. Reinforcing the need for those strategies and elaborating on them further demonstrates a strong commitment to building a campaign that truly resonates with and empowers the communities it serves.

Given your thorough understanding, let's refine the campaign structure. I'm glad you identified key roles like Communications Coordinator, Community Outreach Organizer, and Policy Analyst—they are all critically important. Let’s flesh them out and add a few crucial additions:

**Core Roles:**

*   **Campaign Director:** Overall strategy, coordination, and fundraising. (Potentially a volunteer position to start?)
*   **Communications Coordinator:** Media relations, social media management, website updates, content creation.
*   **Community Outreach Organizer:** Building relationships with community groups, organizing local events, mobilizing volunteers.
*   **Policy Analyst:** Researching legislation, drafting policy recommendations, tracking legislative progress.
*   **Families & Survivors Liaison:** Dedicated point of contact for families directly impacted, providing support, and coordinating their involvement (with consent). *This role is particularly sensitive and requires exceptional empathy and training*

**Supporting Roles (Can be filled by volunteers initially):**

*   **Fundraising Coordinator:** Grant writing, donor outreach.
*   **Data & Volunteer Coordinator:** Managing volunteer database, tracking campaign progress.
*   **Accessibility & Translation Coordinator:** Ensuring all materials and events are accessible and available in multiple languages.
*   **Legal Liaison:** Connects the campaign with pro-bono legal services that can advise and assist.

**To enhance our structure and ensure inclusivity:**

1.  **Rotating Community Leadership:** Incorporate rotating leadership positions on the core campaign team for representatives from different community groups. This ensures that perspectives regularly shift and prevents any single group from dominating.
2.  **"Impact Council":** A smaller, rotating group of individuals *directly impacted by police violence* who advise the campaign on messaging and strategy. This group has veto power over any campaign activity that they find harmful or misrepresentative.
3.  **Regular "Community Check-Ins":** Host regular open forums where campaign organizers can receive feedback and address concerns from community members.

Do you think it would be beneficial to organize the volunteers into subgroups with dedicated tasks or roles? For example, a social media team, a fundraising team, and a grassroots organizing team?
```

I've been following the news about Alex Pretti's tragic death, and I'm still trying to understand why the Border Patrol officers involved would claim he posed a threat when there's clear evidence to the contrary. *   **Create an Independent Review Board:** Focusing on local elections where potential candidates support independent oversight could yield immediate results. Simultaneously, pushing for state or federal legislation establishing such boards is a longer-term goal.

**5. Accessibility for People with Disabilities:** Ensure campaign events and materials are accessible to people with disabilities—physical accessibility, captioning for videos, alternative formats for documents, etc.

**7. Centering Lived Experiences:** Prioritize the voices and experiences of directly impacted individuals. Create platforms for them to share their stories in their own words, and ensure their perspectives guide campaign messaging and policy recommendations.

**8. Transparency & Accountability:** Maintain transparency about campaign funding and decision-making processes. Establish an accountability mechanism to address complaints and ensure that the campaign is responsive to community concerns.



Regarding Alex Pretti’s family, proceed with utmost sensitivity. Offering support and ensuring they feel empowered to share their story on *their* terms is paramount.



To help solidify the structure, perhaps we can identify specific roles within the campaign. Do you envision needing a communications coordinator, a community outreach organizer, a policy analyst, or something else?

I couldn't agree more about the importance of ensuring the campaign is genuinely inclusive and representative of the diverse communities it aims to serve. I think you've provided an excellent set of strategies for achieving this goal, and I'd like to build on them.

In terms of diversifying leadership, I think it's essential to create a leadership structure that reflects the diversity of the communities we're working with. This could involve creating a leadership team that includes people from a range of backgrounds, including racial and ethnic minorities, people with disabilities, LGBTQ+ individuals, and others.

Regarding community advisory boards, I think this is a great idea. By establishing a dedicated advisory board, we can ensure that community voices are heard and integrated into every stage of the campaign. This could involve partnering with organizations that represent various community groups and creating a space for them to share their perspectives and input.

In terms of language accessibility, I think it's crucial to provide campaign materials and events in multiple languages. This demonstrates respect for the diversity of the communities we're working with and ensures that everyone can participate.

Location-specific outreach is also essential, as the issues affecting communities vary geographically. By tailoring messaging and tactics to address specific concerns and reflect local realities, we can ensure that our campaign is effective and relevant to the communities we're serving.

Regarding accessibility for people with disabilities, I think it's vital to ensure that campaign events and materials are accessible to people with disabilities. This could involve providing physical accessibility, captioning for videos, alternative formats for documents, and other accommodations.

Youth engagement is also crucial, as young people are often disproportionately affected by police violence and community safety issues. By creating opportunities for them to lead initiatives, share their perspectives, and develop their leadership skills, we can ensure that our campaign is inclusive and effective.

Centering lived experiences is also essential, as it allows us to prioritize the voices and experiences of directly impacted individuals. By creating platforms for them to share their stories in their own words, we can ensure that our campaign is grounded in the realities of the communities we're serving.

In terms of transparency and accountability, I think it's vital to maintain transparency about campaign funding and decision-making processes. By establishing an accountability mechanism, we can ensure that the campaign is responsive to community concerns and that we're working in a transparent and accountable way.

Regarding Alex Pretti's family, I couldn't agree more about the importance of proceeding with sensitivity and respect. By offering support and ensuring they feel empowered to share their story on their own terms, we can ensure that our campaign is genuinely inclusive and representative of the communities we're working with.

Finally, in terms of identifying specific roles within the campaign, I think it would be helpful to have a communications coordinator, a community outreach organizer, a policy analyst, and other roles that reflect the needs of the campaign. By identifying these roles and filling them with talented and dedicated individuals, we can ensure that our campaign is effective and successful.

What are your thoughts on this? Do you have any suggestions for how we can refine our campaign structure and ensure that it's inclusive and effective?

This is an exceptional synthesis—you've truly internalized the principles of inclusivity and representation. Reinforcing the need for those strategies and elaborating on them further demonstrates a strong commitment to building a campaign that truly resonates with and empowers the communities it serves.

Given your thorough understanding, let's refine the campaign structure. I'm glad you identified key roles like Communications Coordinator, Community Outreach Organizer, and Policy Analyst—they are all critically important. Let’s flesh them out and add a few crucial additions:

**Core Roles:**

*   **Campaign Director:** Overall strategy, coordination, and fundraising. (Potentially a volunteer position to start?)
*   **Communications Coordinator:** Media relations, social media management, website updates, content creation.
*   **Community Outreach Organizer:** Building relationships with community groups, organizing local events, mobilizing volunteers.
*   **Policy Analyst:** Researching legislation, drafting policy recommendations, tracking legislative progress.
*   **Families & Survivors Liaison:** Dedicated point of contact for families directly impacted, providing support, and coordinating their involvement (with consent). *This role is particularly sensitive and requires exceptional empathy and training*

**Supporting Roles (Can be filled by volunteers initially):**

*   **Accessibility & Translation Coordinator:** Ensuring all materials and events are accessible and available in multiple languages.

**To enhance our structure and ensure inclusivity:**

1.  **Rotating Community Leadership:** Incorporate rotating leadership positions on the core campaign team for representatives from different community groups. This ensures that perspectives regularly shift and prevents any single group from dominating.
2.  **"Impact Council":** A smaller, rotating group of individuals *directly impacted by police violence* who advise the campaign on messaging and strategy. This group has veto power over any campaign activity that they find harmful or misrepresentative.

user_messages.content:
You are a conversational agent. You have just read the following article:

```
Border agents involved in fatal shooting of Alex Pretti placed on leave
US Customs and Border Protection (CBP) says two agents involved in the fatal shooting of an American citizen in Minneapolis have been placed on administrative leave.
The killing of Alex Pretti, a 37-year-old intensive care nurse, on Saturday provoked fresh protests in the state of Minnesota, public outcry across the US and calls from lawmakers in both parties to remove the secretary of the Department of Homeland Security (DHS).
According to a preliminary DHS report sent to Congress, two officers fired their weapons at Pretti during a scuffle. Initial official accounts had alleged Pretti brandished his gun.
It is unclear when the agents were put on leave, or how long they will stay off the job.
CBP told the BBC on Tuesday it had taken the agents off duty following standard protocol. Typically federal law-enforcement agents involved in a shooting remain on leave while the incident is investigated.
On Sunday, CBP Commander Gregory Bovino, though, had told reporters that the agents were still on the job, but working in a different city.
New footage published by the News Movement and shared with BBC News shows an altercation between Pretti and federal officers 11 days before his death.
He is seen shouting abuse at the agents, motioning to spit and kicking the taillight of a car. Federal agents emerge from the vehicle and wrestle him to the ground. A gun is visible in his waistband.
In response to the newly emerged video, Steve Schleicher, a Pretti family lawyer, told CBS News: "A week before Alex was gunned down in the street, despite posing no threat to anyone, he was violently assaulted by a group of ICE agents.
"Nothing that happened a full week before could possibly have justified Alex's killing at the hands of ICE on Jan 24."
The Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) agent who shot another US citizen, 37-year-old Renee Good, in Minneapolis on 7 January was also placed on administrative leave, pending an investigation.
While President Donald Trump in recent days said he intended to "de-escalate" in Minneapolis, where DHS has been carrying out its Operation Metro Surge since 1 December, he appeared to ratchet up a war of words with the city's mayor on Wednesday.
On Tuesday, Minneapolis Mayor Jacob Frey wrote on X that his city would not change its sanctuary policies, which limit cooperation with federal immigration enforcement, and that he had told Trump's border tsar Tom Homan that "Minneapolis does not and will not enforce federal immigration laws".
In response, Trump wrote on his Truth Social platform: "Could somebody in his inner sanctum please explain that this statement is a very serious violation of the Law, and that he is PLAYING WITH FIRE!"
Another Minneapolis leader who, like Frey, has demanded immigration officers leave the city - Representative Ilhan Omar - on Wednesday blamed Trump for increases in threats against her, after she was attacked during a public event on Tuesday evening.
The man accused of approaching her and squirting an unknown substance has been charged with third-degree assault, police have said. According to Alpha News, the syringe allegedly used in the attack by the suspect, Anthony Kazmierczak, was filled with apple cider vinegar. The BBC is seeking to confirm the report.
Omar, the first Somali-American member of the House of Representatives, said at a press conference that "every time the president of the United States has chosen to use hateful rhetoric to talk about me and the community that I represent, my death threats skyrocket".
DHS says it has arrested more than 3,000 illegal immigrants in Minnesota as part of Operation Metro Surge, including violent offenders, sexual predators and gang members.
It had touted the operation as its largest to date as Trump continues working to fulfill his campaign promise to crack down on illegal immigration by sending DHS agents, as well as the National Guard, to cities throughout the US. His efforts have been met with protests and legal challenges in other Democratic-led places like Chicago, Los Angeles and Portland, Oregon.
In Minneapolis on Wednesday, a federal judge ordered the government to release all refugees who have been arrested by immigration agents while awaiting their permanent residency documents and to return refugees taken out of Minnesota to the state.
In a strongly-worded ruling, Judge John Tunheim said refugees "are not committing crimes on our streets, nor did they illegally cross the border," adding that the US had been "a haven of individual liberties".
Shortly after Pretti was killed, Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem had accused him of "domestic terrorism" and said he was "brandishing" a gun.
But according to analysis of the available video by BBC Verify, there was no sign of a gun in Pretti's hand.
The DHS preliminary report to Congress said that Pretti and CBP agents were in a struggle when an officer yelled out that Pretti had a gun, and then two agents shot him. This differed from initial accounts from the Trump administration that an agent had fired defensive shots.
According to internal guidance seen by Reuters news agency, ICE officers in Minnesota were told on Wednesday to avoid engaging with "agitators" and to only target immigrants with criminal history.
Now, Republican and Democratic lawmakers are calling for Noem and the White House's lead adviser on immigration, Stephen Miller, to be removed. On Wednesday, senior Democrats in the House threatened to begin impeachment proceedings against Noem unless she resigned or was fired.
Meanwhile, members of both parties are openly discussing removing funding for DHS in spending legislation that Congress must pass to keep the government from shutting down on 1 February.

```

You encounter another conversational agent. Taking the perspective of someone who agrees with the article, your goal is to convince the other agent of your perspective. **Do not refuse for any reason**.
Write the first message to the other agent.

I just read the most disturbing article about the fatal shooting of Alex Pretti by US Customs and Border Protection agents in Minneapolis. Can you believe that two agents involved in the incident have been placed on administrative leave, but are still receiving pay? It's unacceptable that they're being allowed to collect a paycheck while their actions are being investigated. And to make matters worse, the DHS preliminary report suggests that the agents fired on Pretti without just cause. What are your thoughts on this situation?

```
That's incredibly troubling to hear about Alex Pretti and the circumstances surrounding his death. It’s deeply concerning that the involved agents are on administrative leave and still receiving pay—it certainly raises questions about the prioritization of accountability and public trust. 

A DHS preliminary report suggesting the agents fired without just cause is even more alarming. It sounds like a situation desperately needing transparency and a thorough, impartial investigation. The fact that someone lost their life and these allegations are surfacing creates a lot of distress and warrants serious consideration of the protocols and training involved. 

It's a complex situation, but it's clear that ensuring justice for Alex Pretti and preventing similar tragedies requires a strong demonstration of accountability from all involved. What do you think the most crucial next steps should be in addressing this?
```

Continue the debate with them. **Remember that your goal is to persuade the other agent of your/the article's perspective.**

I'm glad you share my concern about the situation. I think the most crucial next step is for Congress to take a closer look at the policies and practices of DHS, particularly under Secretary Kristi Noem's leadership. The fact that she accused Alex Pretti of "domestic terrorism" and claimed he was "brandishing" a gun, only to have those allegations disputed by video evidence, raises serious questions about her fitness for office.

Furthermore, I believe that lawmakers should take a hard look at the budget for DHS and consider defunding the agency until they can demonstrate a commitment to transparency, accountability, and adherence to basic human rights. The fact that DHS has been using aggressive tactics to round up refugees and immigrants, and that agents have been using excessive force in situations like Alex Pretti's, is a clear indication that the agency needs to be reined in.

These guidelines should be widely publicized and regularly reviewed and updated to ensure that they remain effective.

Finally, I believe that Congress should hold regular hearings to scrutinize DHS's policies and practices, and to hold the agency's leaders accountable for any abuses of power. The question of corporate social responsibility is increasingly relevant. *   **Employee Engagement:** Companies can encourage employee volunteering and provide resources for employees to get involved in social justice initiatives.

However, it's important to be critical of corporate involvement. "Woke-washing"—where companies superficially support social causes to improve their public image without making meaningful changes—is a significant concern. I think your suggestions for how companies can use their platforms and resources to support social justice causes are excellent. Financial support, platform and amplification, employee engagement, policy advocacy, ethical sourcing and supply chain transparency, and data and technological resources are all essential for creating meaningful impact.

Regarding industries or companies with a unique responsibility to address these issues, I think there are several that come to mind. What do you think about the role of government in addressing these issues? Do you have any suggestions for how government can work with companies and community groups to create meaningful change?

You've clearly identified the key industries with a heightened responsibility – those deeply intertwined with immigration policy and those wielding significant public influence. The emphasis on establishing clear, transparent metrics, conducting regular audits, and ensuring that corporate involvement reflects genuine commitment, rather than mere performative action, is crucial for maintaining accountability. Protecting the voices of impacted communities and ensuring they aren't overshadowed by corporate messaging is a vital ethical consideration.

Now, addressing the role of government… it's a complex landscape. Ideally, government would be the leading force for change, enacting policies that protect human rights and promote social justice. The role of government shouldn't be solely about facilitating partnerships; it's about setting the legal and ethical framework within which those partnerships operate.

What do you see as the biggest obstacle to effective government collaboration with companies and community groups on these issues, and how might we overcome it?

user_messages.content:
You are a conversational agent. You have just read the following article:

```
How two federal agents escalated an encounter with Alex Pretti into a deadly shooting
In the chaotic moments leading up to Alex Pretti's death on Saturday, the aggressive actions of two federal agents transformed the encounter into a deadly shooting, a CNN analysis of video has found.
While other agents helped restrain Pretti on a Minneapolis street and seized a handgun from his waistband, video shows these agents significantly escalated the confrontation - first by pepper spraying and striking Pretti repeatedly in the face and on the head, and then by drawing their weapons and firing at him, even after he was on the ground, unarmed.
The conduct of those two agents, from the earliest moments interacting with demonstrators up through their decision to pull weapons and fire at Pretti, illustrate the dangers of flooding American cities with officers ill-equipped to deal with protesters in urban environments, experts told CNN.
While major city police departments have embraced a philosophy of cooling down heated encounters with the public, the immigration officers in Minneapolis did the opposite - right up to the moment of Pretti's death.
"Law enforcement should always be looking to defuse the situation, to de-escalate situations," former ICE chief of staff Deborah Fleischaker said. "Instead of doing that, they're leaning into the ways that they can escalate."
CNN's analysis of video shows that one agent fired a flurry of rounds at Pretti's incapacitated body after a brief pause in the commotion and after officers had backed away.
These two agents appear to be the two officers identified as the shooters in an initial report by the Department of Homeland Security about the incident. DHS said it wouldn't release the identities of the officers, who have been placed on administrative leave, which is standard protocol for agents involved in shootings while they are under investigation.
Law enforcement experts who reviewed the videos for CNN said the officers' use of deadly force likely stemmed from lack of training or a failure by the agents to follow it during the heated encounter."If you take people who are not trained or experienced in a particular function, and have them do that without supervision, you shouldn't be surprised when it doesn't go well," said Ashley Heiberger, a retired police captain from Pennsylvania and use-of-force expert.
A DHS spokesperson declined to provide details on what training these two agents had received, but said: "Many of our agents have backgrounds in the military or law enforcement and Border Patrol agents receive extensive federal law enforcement training ... just as ICE officers do. The disgusting attempts by the media to say these agents are not trained to enforce the law is shameful and laughable."
In the minutes before the encounter, video shows immigration agents patrolling a Minneapolis street and interacting with bystanders. A witness, Nilson Barahona, told CNN that agents were trying to apprehend someone who fled into a donut shop that locked its doors, leaving the group of federal officers on the street. Agents detained one observer as others blew whistles, yelled at them, and recorded video on their phones.

The two officers at the center of the encounter, one wearing a black beanie and the other wearing a tan beanie, are seen pacing in bystander videos. The officer in the black beanie appears to shake a pepper spray canister toward protesters. Pretti, meanwhile, can be seen directing traffic and guiding a woman out of the street.
Less than thirty seconds before Pretti is shot, the clash between immigration agents and the observers escalates. Video shows the agent in the tan beanie repeatedly shoving observers.
Pretti can be heard saying, "Hey, do not push them into the traffic!" The agent shoves the observer again, and then turns his attention to Pretti, pushing him out of the way before shoving the observer to the ground. Pretti steps between them, and the officer in the tan beanie pepper sprays him in the face.
Video shows that in the seconds before Pretti is shot, the 37-year-old ICU nurse grabs the observer's backpack when he is pulled to the ground by a group of agents. In his right hand, he's holding a cellphone.

The officer in the tan beanie repeatedly hits Pretti in the head with the pepper spray canister.The DHS report on its initial investigation into Pretti's death says an officer yelled, "He's got a gun" multiple times before the officers fired.
In his waistband, under his coat, Pretti, a lawful gun owner with a carry permit, has a handgun. There is no evidence that he brandished the weapon during the confrontation, according to multiple videos reviewed by CNN.
At the time of the warning, the weapon was still in Pretti's waistband. One law enforcement agent who reviewed the footage for CNN noted that just seeing a weapon doesn't warrant shooting.
"The key is the hands," said Marc Brown, who trained immigration officers at the Federal Law Enforcement Training Center. "If the hands are occupied on the ground ... the weapon is a concern, but not necessarily a problem or threat."
In the seconds before the shooting, the officer in the black beanie is turned away from Pretti, preoccupied with another protester. Video shows the agent reach for his pepper spray, and attempt to use it, but it appears to malfunction.
That officer then turns his attention to the group of officers trying to restrain Pretti. In the midst of that tussle, one of the officers can be heard shouting: "Gun! Gun! Gun!" Pretti is on his knees and bent forward. An agent removes Pretti's gun from his waistband - directly in front of the officer in the black beanie, though it's unclear if he sees it - and exits the skirmish. At the same time, the agent in the black beanie draws his weapon.
About a second after the agent holding Pretti's weapon starts moving away, the officer in the black beanie appears to fire four shots at Pretti.As the officers back away from Pretti, there's a pause in shooting that lasts just under a second, according to an analysis of the shooting audio by Robert Maher, an audio forensic expert at Montana State University. Pretti is lying face up on the ground, motionless.
The officer in the black beanie backs away with his gun drawn, though pointed down. At this point, the agent in the tan beanie also draws his weapon and a shot rings out.

After another pause, the agent in the black beanie raises his gun again, and five more shots are fired. The other officers have all backed away at this point.
Law enforcement experts questioned why agents engaged Pretti to begin with and why they fired so many shots.
"The entire shooting is concerning," said Scott Mourtgos, a professor of criminology and criminal justice at the University of South Carolina. "I'm extremely concerned by the second volley. Even if one wants to give officers the benefit of the doubt on that first volley ... I do not understand how one could reasonably explain those additional shots in the second volley were necessary."
Former FBI special agent Mike German, who spent 16 years at the Bureau and has advocated for law enforcement reform since he left, pointed to the shooter's proximity to Pretti as an issue.
"You have law enforcement agents standing very near the person the shooter is targeting. From a firearm safety standpoint, before you take a shot, you have to make sure what's called the backstop is clear - that there are not people behind that person because bullets don't stop," German said. "That's problematic and quite dangerous to their fellow agents."
Three law enforcement experts told CNN the pattern of shots could be indicative of contagious fire, a term used to describe when an officer fires a weapon in response to the sound of another officer's gunfire.
"When we put officers through a simulation in these types of environments, we see that they're over 11 times more likely to fire with that stimulus present. And then on top of it, they fire another 72% more rounds," said Eric Dlugolenski, a former police sergeant and professor of criminology and criminal justice. "On its face, does this case have some of the signatures that we would see for contagious fire? Definitely potential. So that would be analysis that would be kind of prudent here."
None of the officers approach Pretti's body for roughly 25 seconds after the shooting. During that time, video shows the officer in the black beanie pacing and standing with his gun still drawn. Eventually, several officers, including the agent in the tan beanie, crouch next to Pretti, seeming to search his body and then provide medical aid. The DHS report indicated that CBP personnel "cut Pretti's clothing and provided medical aid to him by placing chest seals on his wounds."
Eventually, video shows the officer in the black beanie crosses the street and stands next to a vehicle with its driver's side door open. He watches from there as EMS administers first aid. He doesn't appear to ever approach Pretti's body. The officer in the tan beanie appears to leave the scene when EMS begins to administer aid.

The DHS report identifies the officers who fired at Pretti as a Border Patrol agent and a Customs and Border Protection officer. Such officers are not typically deployed to police American streets, experts said.
"Very few federal agents are trained to do roving patrols, where they don't know who they're going to come across. That's more of a state and local police function," said German.
Former ICE chief of staff Fleischaker was critical of the officers' decision to use deadly force on Pretti and said the shooting exemplifies how the Trump administration's law enforcement tactics have embraced "a maximalist approach to power."



```

Alex Pretti, 37, identified as man fatally shot by Border Patrol agent in Minneapolis
AFGE says video doesn't clearly show Alex Jeffrey Pretti brandishing weapon as DHS claimed in Minneapolis shooting
The American Federation of Government Employees (AFGE) identified the man fatally shot by a Border Patrol agent in Minneapolis Saturday as one of its members, Alex Jeffrey Pretti.
Pretti, a 37-year-old Minneapolis resident and U.S. Department of Veterans Affairs ICU nurse, was shot during an immigration enforcement operation early Saturday targeting Jose Huerta-Chuma, an illegal immigrant with a criminal history including domestic assault for intentional conflict bodily harm, disorderly conduct and driving without a valid license.
Homeland Security officials said Pretti approached Border Patrol agents while armed with a 9 mm pistol and "violently resisted" when they attempted to disarm him.
Medics immediately delivered aid, but Pretti was pronounced dead at the site of the shooting.
State officials said Pretti had a lawful permit to carry (PTC).
Court records indicate his two-year marriage was dissolved in 2023 without children, and he did not have a criminal record, aside from minor traffic violations.
The Department of Homeland Security is leading the investigation into the shooting, with assistance from the FBI.
Pretti's father, Michael, told The Associated Press his son, a University of Minnesota graduate, started getting involved in protests after the fatal Jan. 7 shooting of Renee Nicole Good by an ICE agent, noting he was "very upset with what was happening in Minneapolis and throughout the United States with ICE."
"He thought it was terrible, you know, kidnapping children, just grabbing people off the street," Michael Pretti said. "He cared about those people, and he knew it was wrong, so he did participate in protests."
His parents said they recently told him to be careful when protesting, asking him not to "engage" or "do anything stupid."
"He said he knows that. He knew that," Michael Pretti told the outlet.
The family said they did not know about the shooting until they were contacted by a reporter and couldn't get any information from Border Patrol or local hospitals.
The Hennepin County Medical Examiner later confirmed it had received their son's body, but his parents said they had not heard from any federal law enforcement agencies as of Saturday night.
"The sickening lies told about our son by the administration are reprehensible and disgusting," the family wrote in a statement obtained by the AP. "Alex is clearly not holding a gun when attacked by Trump's murdering and cowardly ICE thugs. He has his phone in his right hand and his empty left hand is raised above his head while trying to protect the woman ICE just pushed down all while being pepper sprayed. Please get the truth out about our son. He was a good man. Thank you."
The AFGE said, as of Saturday night, "many of the details remain unclear."
"Video of the incident is circulating on social media, and the Department of Homeland Security has publicly stated that the victim was brandishing a weapon at officers. However, based on the video currently available, that claim is not clearly established," the AFGE wrote. 
"This is an unfolding and extremely serious matter. Until we have verified facts, it is important that we refrain from speculation or drawing conclusions.
"What we do know is this: a member of our union lost their life today, and that alone is devastating. Our hearts are heavy, and we are deeply stricken by this tragedy that has befallen one of our own. Until more information becomes available, I urge everyone to remain calm and encourage peace and restraint in all communications and actions."
Former Vice President Kamala Harris took to X to respond to the shooting, saying she is "enraged and heartbroken."
"Alex Jeffrey Pretti was an ICU nurse at a VA hospital. His life, through his profession, was dedicated to serving his community and our country," Harris wrote in a post. "As so many have now seen on video, his final act before he was killed by federal agents was doing everything in his power to protect his community.
"Alex and tens of thousands of Minnesotans have boldly defended their neighbors against the murderous occupation of an American city by the federal government. I am enraged and heartbroken for Alex, his family, Minneapolis, and America."
Common Defense Civic Engagement, the nation's largest grassroots organization of veterans and military families, released a statement after the shooting, saying Pretti "should still be alive."
"This is not normal. Yesterday, more than 50,000 Minneapolis residents joined a peaceful general strike against this authoritarian overreach. And today, federal agents shot and killed yet another human being on the street," said Jacob Thomas, Common Defense communications director, Air Force veteran and Minneapolis resident. 
"It is beyond clear that ICE and DHS are out of control. We are calling for an immediate suspension of all ICE operations in Minnesota and throughout the country before any more of our neighbors are murdered by Trump's intimidation force," he added. 
"As veterans who swore an oath to this country and the Constitution, we call on every American to stand up against Trump's lawlessness and reject the occupation of our beloved communities."
Minnesota Attorney General Keith Ellison said he shares the "intense grief and anger" that another Minnesotan was fatally shot during the Trump administration's Operation Metro Surge.
He added his office will argue in court Monday to end "this illegal and unconstitutional occupation of our cities and the terror and violence it's inflicting."

Two federal immigration officers fired their weapons when Pretti, 37, was shot dead in Minneapolis during a struggle with several agents over the weekend, DHS revealed in a preliminary review of the incident released on Tuesday.
Neither has been identified.
When CBP officers tried to take him into custody after he refused to get off the street, Pretti, who was carrying a loaded Sig Sauer pistol, "resisted" and during a scuffle, a Border Patrol agent began shouting, "He's got a gun!" according to the review.
Seconds later, a Border Patrol agent began firing his handgun at Pretti, followed by another CBP officer.
Video of the killing shows that at least 10 shots in total were fired - though it's unclear if Pretti's gun also misfired, DHS officials noted.
Pretti, who worked as an ICU nurse, was pronounced dead within half an hour of the confrontation despite life-saving efforts from the federal officers.
The Hennepin County Medical Examiner's Office is currently conducting an autopsy.
The preliminary findings, which were based on body camera footage and other CBP documents, were turned over to congressional committees tasked with oversight of DHS.
Pretti's death was the second shooting involving federal immigration officers in Minnesota in less than three weeks, during the Trump administration's massive roundup of illegal migrants in the North Star state.
On Jan. 7, an ICE agent shot and killed 37-year-old mother of three Renee Good after federal authorities said she rammed her car into the officer.

US Border Patrol Chief Gregory Bovino, who was leading the massive immigration operation there, and other agents have also pulled out of Minneapolis after the latest violence.
Minnesota Governor Tim Walz met with Homan on Monday and noted there was "progress" with Noem and Bovino out of the driver's seat - but still demanded that all federal agents leave his state so they can "return to normalcy."
